notes: Catalog of device/data providers for direct-API integrations. Clean-room: compiled from public developer documentation only. Where no official open API exists the provider is marked export_only and the honest workaround (file export or local bridge) is described instead of pretending an API exists.

providers.label: WHOOP
key_steps: Go to developer.whoop.com and sign in with your WHOOP account, then open the Developer Dashboard.
Create a Team (required once), then 'Create App' (up to 5 apps per account).
Select scopes: read:profile, read:recovery, read:cycles, read:sleep (read:workout optional).
Add a redirect URL, e.g. http://localhost:8765/callback — it must match your OAuth request exactly.
Copy the Client ID and Client Secret from the app page (secret is server-side only, never log it).
Export OPENHEALTH_WHOOP_CLIENT_ID, OPENHEALTH_WHOOP_CLIENT_SECRET, OPENHEALTH_WHOOP_REDIRECT_URI, then run 'openhealth whoop-auth-url' and 'openhealth whoop-exchange-code' to finish the OAuth flow.
providers.rate_limit_note: Per-app limits (defaults around 100 requests/min and 10,000/day). Daily sync needs only a handful of calls.

providers.label: Oura Ring
key_steps: Sign in at cloud.ouraring.com with your Oura account.
Open 'My Applications' at cloud.ouraring.com/oauth/applications and create a new application.
Set a redirect URI (acts as a whitelist for OAuth callbacks), e.g. http://localhost:8765/callback.
Copy the Client ID and Client Secret from the application page.
Run the OAuth2 code flow against api.ouraring.com; send 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' on /v2/usercollection/* endpoints.
Note: legacy Personal Access Tokens were deprecated in December 2025 — OAuth2 is now the only auth method.
providers.rate_limit_note: Per-token rate limits enforced by the v2 API; polling daily summaries once a day stays far below them.
providers.connector: openhealth/connectors/oura.py (file export today; live OAuth2 pull planned)

providers.label: Garmin
key_steps: Official Health API requires an approved application to the Garmin Connect Developer Program at developer.garmin.com (reviewed in ~2 business days).
Apply as a company/institution with a company-domain email — Garmin grants the program for business use, not personal hobby projects.
Once approved you get portal access, an evaluation environment and consumer key/secret for the OAuth flow; the Health API is primarily push/webhook based.
Personal no-approval fallback (what our connector uses today): account.garmin.com → 'Export Your Data', or Garmin Connect per-metric CSV exports, then feed the files to connectors/garmin.py.
providers.rate_limit_note: Evaluation keys carry reduced quotas; production Health API is push-based so polling limits rarely matter.

providers.label: Fitbit (Google)
key_steps: Sign in at dev.fitbit.com with the Google account your Fitbit data lives under.
Go to dev.fitbit.com/apps → 'Register a new app'.
Choose OAuth application type 'Personal' — this unlocks intraday time-series for your own account without extra approval.
Set a redirect URI, e.g. http://localhost:8765/callback, and save to get Client ID and Client Secret.
Run the OAuth2 Authorization Code flow with PKCE; request scopes like sleep, heartrate, activity, weight, oxygen_saturation.
providers.rate_limit_note: 150 requests/hour per user token — batch daily pulls and cache aggressively.

providers.label: Polar
key_steps: Sign in at admin.polaraccesslink.com with your regular Polar Flow account (any Flow user can create a client).
Click 'Create client' and fill in application details plus an authorization redirect URL.
Copy the Client ID and Client Secret shown after creation.
Run the OAuth2 code flow, then register the user against the AccessLink /v3/users endpoint before pulling data.
providers.rate_limit_note: AccessLink documents per-user request quotas; most data is fetched via transaction endpoints which are inherently batched.

providers.label: Suunto
key_steps: Register a developer account at apizone.suunto.com (Azure API Management portal).
Subscribe to the Development API product; after acceptance your primary/secondary subscription keys appear under Profile → 'Your subscriptions'.
Edit the OAuth settings in your profile to register your app (name, redirect URL) — the authorization server is cloudapi-oauth.suunto.com.
Send the Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key header on every Cloud API request (not needed on the OAuth endpoints) plus the user's Bearer token.
providers.rate_limit_note: Development subscription has reduced request limits; production keys are granted after review.

providers.label: COROS
key_steps: There is no self-serve portal: submit an API application through the COROS Help Center form (link above) describing your use case.
Wait for review/approval by the COROS open-platform team.
After approval COROS issues OAuth2 credentials and the API documentation/base URL for your account.
No-approval fallback: COROS app → export activities as FIT files and ingest those.
providers.rate_limit_note: Quotas are communicated with the approved application; not publicly documented.

providers.label: Amazfit / Zepp
key_steps: Honest status: the Zepp Open Platform (dev.huami.com) targets enterprise partners; there is no self-serve personal-data REST API. Zepp OS developer docs cover on-watch apps, not cloud data access.
Practical route 1: Zepp app → Profile → Settings → 'Export data' (GDPR export) and ingest the files.
Practical route 2: pair the watch with Gadgetbridge (open-source, local BLE, no cloud) and export its database.
Practical route 3: let Zepp sync to another platform you can read (e.g. Google/Health Connect on-device).
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — file export / local bridge only.

providers.label: Xiaomi (Mi Fitness / Mi Band)
key_steps: Honest status: Xiaomi offers no official open API for Mi Fitness wearable data.
Route 1: Mi Fitness app GDPR data export (Profile → Settings → request data export), then ingest the archive.
Route 2: pair the band with Gadgetbridge — fully local BLE, exportable SQLite database, no Xiaomi cloud at all.
Route 3 (sensors, not wearables): Xiaomi/Aqara home sensors integrate cleanly into Home Assistant (BLE/Zigbee), then read them via the Home Assistant API — see the home_assistant entry.
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — local bridge / export only.

providers.label: Samsung Health
key_steps: Honest status: the Samsung Health Data SDK is on-device Android only, and the server-to-server data API is partner-only (requires a Samsung partnership agreement). No self-serve cloud API for individuals.
Practical route 1: Samsung Health app → Settings → 'Download personal data' and ingest the export.
Practical route 2: sync Samsung Health to Health Connect on-device and export from there.
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — partner API only; personal access is export-based.

providers.label: Apple Health
key_steps: Honest status: there is no Apple Health cloud API by design — HealthKit runs on-device only. A future on-device companion could read HealthKit directly; today we use the export.
On iPhone: Health app → tap your profile picture → 'Export All Health Data' → share the resulting export.zip.
Unzip and feed export.xml to connectors/apple_health.py — it maps records to canonical Observations.
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — file export (export grows large; expect hundreds of MB after years of data).

providers.label: Google Health Connect
key_steps: Honest status: Health Connect is an on-device Android API with no cloud REST equivalent; the old Google Fit REST API is deprecated and being shut down, so do not build on it.
Practical route 1: use an on-device exporter app (e.g. 'Health Connect Export') to dump Health Connect data to files, then ingest.
Practical route 2: the Home Assistant companion app can push selected Android sensors to your Home Assistant instance — then read via the home_assistant entry.
Note: Fitbit cloud data is NOT behind Health Connect — use the fitbit entry (dev.fitbit.com) for direct API access.
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — on-device API, local export only.

providers.label: Home Assistant
key_steps: Why it matters: Home Assistant is the universal local bridge — Xiaomi/Aqara sensors, HomeKit/Eve devices, BLE scales and air monitors all become readable entities with full history, no vendor cloud needed.
In your Home Assistant UI: click your user name (bottom left) → 'Security' tab → 'Long-lived access tokens' → 'Create token'. Copy it immediately — it is shown once and valid 10 years.
Set OPENHEALTH_HOME_ASSISTANT_URL to your instance (e.g. http://homeassistant.local:8123) and OPENHEALTH_HOME_ASSISTANT_TOKEN to the token.
Read /api/states or /api/history/period/<timestamp> with header 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'. Everything stays on your LAN — a perfect local-first fit.
providers.rate_limit_note: Local instance — no vendor rate limits; be gentle with history queries on large recorder databases.

providers.label: Withings
key_steps: Create a regular Withings account, then sign in at developer.withings.com and open the developer dashboard.
Create an application (public-cloud plan is free for personal use); set a callback/redirect URL.
Copy the Client ID and Consumer Secret from the application page.
Run the OAuth2 web flow (account.withings.com authorize endpoint), then call wbsapi.withings.net endpoints like /measure, /v2/sleep, /v2/heart.
Covers the whole Withings family from one integration: scales (Body+, Body Scan), BPM blood-pressure monitors, Sleep Analyzer mat, ScanWatch.
providers.rate_limit_note: Documented per-client limit around 120 requests/minute — generous for daily syncs.

providers.label: Omron
key_steps: Honest status: Omron's wellness/data API is partner-only (business agreements); there is no self-serve developer portal for personal data.
Practical route: OMRON connect app → history → export readings as CSV/PDF and ingest the CSV.
Alternative: prefer a Withings BPM device if you want blood pressure over a real open API (see the withings entry).
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — file export only.

providers.label: Eufy (smart scales)
key_steps: Honest status: Anker/Eufy publishes no API for EufyLife scale data.
Practical route 1: EufyLife app → data export (CSV via profile/settings) and ingest the file.
Practical route 2: community BLE integrations read some Eufy scales locally into Home Assistant — then use the home_assistant entry.
Alternative: a Withings scale gives the same measurements behind an official OAuth2 API.
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — file export / local bridge only.

providers.label: Dexcom CGM
key_steps: Register a developer account at developer.dexcom.com — no approval gate for sandbox.
Create an app in the portal (name, description, redirect URI) to get Client ID and Client Secret.
Develop against the sandbox first: base URL https://sandbox-api.dexcom.com with simulated CGM users.
For your real data run the OAuth2 code flow against api.dexcom.com (EU accounts: api.eu.dexcom.com) and pull /v3/users/self/egvs.
Know the limitation: the API serves retrospective estimated glucose values with a delay, not a real-time stream.
providers.rate_limit_note: Per-app limits documented on the portal; EGVs update every 5 minutes so frequent polling is pointless anyway.

providers.label: FreeStyle Libre (Abbott)
key_steps: Honest status: Abbott offers no official public API for personal Libre data.
Official route: LibreView (libreview.com) → 'Download glucose data' → CSV export, then ingest.

Unofficial route (use at your own risk, may break, ToS-sensitive): the LibreLinkUp endpoints (api-eu.libreview.io and regional variants) power community tools like nightscout-librelink-up; they authenticate with LibreLinkUp follower credentials.
If you need a supported CGM API, Dexcom is the one with a real developer portal.
providers.rate_limit_note: Unofficial endpoints are undocumented — poll conservatively (every 5+ minutes) if you accept the risk.

providers.label: Levels (and similar CGM apps)
key_steps: Honest status: Levels, January AI and similar metabolic apps are layers on top of Dexcom/Libre sensors and expose no public API of their own.
Practical route: connect the underlying sensor directly (see dexcom / freestyle_libre entries) — you get the raw data those apps are built on.
Some of these apps offer in-app CSV export; if yours does, ingest that file.
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — no public API.

providers.label: Awair
key_steps: Go to developer.getawair.com and log in with your existing Awair account (onboarding flow at /onboard/welcome).
Request an Access Token — Hobbyist-tier tokens are approved automatically.
Copy the token and set OPENHEALTH_AWAIR_TOKEN.
Call e.g. GET /v1/users/self/devices and /v1/users/self/devices/{type}/{id}/air-data/latest with header 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'.
providers.rate_limit_note: Hobbyist tier is quota-limited per day — poll every 15 minutes or less often, not continuously.

providers.label: Airthings
key_steps: Sign in at dashboard.airthings.com, open Integrations → 'API Clients'.
Create a client: name, description, and tick the scope read:device:current_values.
Store the generated Client ID and Client Secret.
Use the OAuth2 client-credentials grant: POST https://accounts-api.airthings.com/v1/token, then call ext-api.airthings.com/v1/devices and .../latest-samples with the Bearer token.
providers.rate_limit_note: Consumer API is rate-limited (on the order of 120 requests/hour per client) — sample every few minutes at most.

providers.label: Netatmo
key_steps: Sign in with your Netatmo account at dev.netatmo.com.
Create an app at dev.netatmo.com/apps/createanapp (name, description, contact) — Client ID and Client Secret are generated immediately.
For personal use the portal's Token Generator can mint a token directly: pick scopes (read_station, read_homecoach) and click 'Generate Token'.
Call api.netatmo.com/api/getstationsdata (Weather Station) or /api/gethomecoachsdata (Healthy Home Coach) with the Bearer token.
providers.rate_limit_note: Per-user and per-app quotas (tens of requests per 10 seconds) — fine for 5-minute polling.

providers.label: Eve / HomeKit devices
key_steps: Honest status: Eve devices are deliberately cloud-free — HomeKit/Thread/Matter only, no vendor REST API exists.
Practical route: pair the device with Home Assistant via the HomeKit Device (homekit_controller) or Matter integration — readings become local entities with history.
Then read them through the Home Assistant REST API (see the home_assistant entry). Fully local chain, very much in the spirit of this project.
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — local protocols only.

providers.label: Eight Sleep
key_steps: Honest status: Eight Sleep publishes no official public API; community projects reverse-engineer the app's cloud API and can break at any time.
Practical route 1: request a personal data export from Eight Sleep support (GDPR/CCPA) and ingest the files.
Practical route 2 (unofficial, at your own risk): community Home Assistant integrations expose sleep stages and bed temperature; read them via the home_assistant entry.
providers.rate_limit_note: Not applicable — no official API; unofficial clients should poll sparingly.
